agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on labour negotiations with six employee groups
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to consider a proposal to enter closed-session labour negotiations with six employee organizations. These groups include unions representing general city workers (SEIU Local 1021), mid-level and executive managers (IFPTE Local 21), police officers (RPOA), police managers (RPMA), firefighters (IAFF Local 188), and fire managers (RFMA). The proposal would allow council representatives to meet privately to discuss wages, benefits, and working conditions. This is a proposed step — no final decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time — this is an internal city labour matter.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
C.1
CONFERENCE WITH LABOR NEGOTIATORS (Government Code Section 54957.6)
Public Comments:
Agency Representatives: Sharrone Taylor, Jack Hughes, and Lisa Charbonneau
Employee organizations:
1. SEIU Local 1021 Full Time Unit and Part Time Unit
2. IFPTE Local 21 Mid-Level Management Unit and Executive Management Units
3. Richmond Police Officers Association RPOA
4. Richmond Police Management Association RPMA
5. IAFF Local 188
6. Richmond Fire Management Association RFMA

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Housing Authority cost allocation plan for FY 2026-2027
agenda_briefs.summary: Council approved a Title 2 CFR 200 compliant cost allocation plan for the Richmond Housing Authority (RHA) and adopted the FY 2026-2027 operating budget. The budget includes $4,154,579 in estimated revenues and $5,439,652 in expenditures, balanced by a $1,260,073 General Fund subsidy and $25,000 in Hacienda proceeds. The cost allocation plan quantifies City costs incurred on behalf of RHA to recover eligible administrative and support costs from HUD. RHA manages 783 affordable housing units (100 at Nystrom Village, 683 affiliated units) and operates with 5.0 FTEs.

key_dates.label: FY 2026-2027 budget and cost allocation plan takes effect

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Housing Authority budget for FY 2026-2027 up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a joint resolution to approve the Richmond Housing Authority (RHA) budget for FY 2026-2027. The budget totals $5,439,652 in expenditures with $4,154,579 in estimated revenues. To cover the $1,285,073 shortfall, the City of Richmond would provide a $1,260,073 General Fund subsidy and $25,000 from Hacienda proceeds. The budget funds 5.0 FTEs, capital improvements at Nystrom Village (including $300,000 for gutter replacement, $320,000 for sidewalk repair, $80,000 for parking lot light repair), and $1,529,058 in equipment and contract services. This is a proposed decision — not yet approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on June 16, 2026 — if approved, this budget will affect city funding for housing programs and capital repairs at Nystrom Village.

key_dates.label: Council vote on RHA FY 2026-2027 budget

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $400K legal services deal
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on a fourth amendment to a legal services agreement with Liebert Cassidy Whitmore. If approved, the contract extends through June 30, 2027, and the total not-to-exceed amount increases by $400,000 to $1,965,000. The firm provides labor relations and police personnel legal support. Hourly rates are: partners $435, senior counsel $365, associates $260–$345, paralegals $145. The amendment also covers labor negotiations for three bargaining groups and the Fire Department's transition to Advanced Life Support services.

key_dates.label: Council vote on proposed legal services amendment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on eScribe software contract extension
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposed sole-source contract with eScribe Software Ltd. for agenda management software. A 'sole-source' contract means the city would hire this one company without getting bids from other companies. The proposed deal would run for three years, from July 1, 2026 to June 30, 2029, and cost no more than $56,251.20 in the first year, with annual price increases of up to eight percent. This software is used to create and publish City Council meeting agendas and packets. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider approving it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from business owners — this is an internal city administration contract. Watch for the council decision.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond zoning changes to meet housing targets
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a bylaw amending the Richmond Municipal Code to implement 11 Housing Element programs. Key changes: minimum density in RM-1, RM-2, CM-1, CM-2, and CM-3 zones increases to 20 dwelling units per acre; parking minimums are eliminated for projects within one-half mile of a major transit stop; maximum building height in RM-1 and RM-2 zones rises from 35 to 38 feet; mixed-use zone heights increase near residential zones (e.g., CM-5 from 35 to 45 ft within 50 ft of a residential zone); group homes are allowed in all residential zones; and design review will rely on objective standards. The ordinance also updates density bonus rules and allows Low Barrier Navigation Centers in multi-family and mixed-use zones.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if passed, these zoning changes could affect your property's development options.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $250K environmental contract for Miraflores
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is voting on zoning and General Plan amendments to implement 11 Housing Element programs. Key changes: minimum density increases to 20 du/ac in RM-1, RM-2, CM-1, CM-2, and CM-3 zones; parking minimums eliminated for any project within 1/2 mile of public transit (AB 2097); building heights in RM-1 and RM-2 increase from 35 to 38 feet; mixed-use zone heights increase near residential zones (e.g., CM-5 from 35 to 45 ft within 50 ft of a residential zone); design review must use objective standards for housing; density bonus ordinance streamlined to reference state law; group homes allowed in all zones permitting residential uses; Low Barrier Navigation Centers allowed in multi-family and mixed-use zones.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the proposed zoning amendments to understand how new density, parking, and height rules may affect your property or development plans. Attend the council meeting or submit comments before the vote.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Housing Element zoning amendments

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on housing equity contract amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council will vote on zoning and General Plan amendments implementing 11 Housing Element programs. Key changes: minimum density increases to 20 du/ac in RM-1, RM-2, CM-1, CM-2, and CM-3 zones; parking minimums eliminated for projects within 1/2 mile of public transit; building heights increase to 38 ft in RM-1/RM-2 zones and up to 45 ft in CM-1/CM-2 zones within 30 ft of residential zones; density bonus ordinance streamlined to reference State law; group homes allowed in all residential zones; Low Barrier Navigation Centers and emergency shelters permitted in multi-family and mixed-use zones.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $2.6M contract with Contra Costa County for encampment resolution
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider adopting zoning and General Plan amendments to implement 11 Housing Element programs. Key changes: increase minimum density to 20 dwelling units per acre in RM-1, RM-2, CM-1, CM-2, and CM-3 zones; eliminate minimum parking requirements for any residential, commercial, or other development within one-half mile of public transit (AB 2097); increase max building height in RM-1 and RM-2 zones from 35 to 38 feet; increase max heights in CM-1 through CM-5 zones near residential zones; allow group homes in all zones permitting residential uses; allow Low Barrier Navigation Centers in multi-family, mixed-use, and nonresidential zones; and update density bonus ordinance to reference state law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on May 19, 2026 — these changes will affect development feasibility, parking requirements, and permitted uses citywide.

key_dates.label: City Council public hearing on zoning amendments

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $150K mental health contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with Health Management Associates, Inc. to conduct a full review of Richmond's mental health and trauma-response services. If approved, the contract would be worth up to $150,000 and run from June 16, 2026, to December 31, 2026. The city manager would also be allowed to make small changes to the contract as long as they stay within the approved budget. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $2M violence prevention grant
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider accepting a $2,034,800 CalVIP grant for 2026-2029 to serve 520 youth aged 14-35 in Richmond and West Contra Costa County. If approved, the city would sign contracts with RPAL ($450,000) for mentoring, life skills training, subsidized employment, and with Evident Change ($100,000) for evaluation. Grant term: April 1, 2026 – September 30, 2029. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from business owners at this time. Watch for the council decision — this could affect community safety programs.

key_dates.label: CalVIP grant program start date

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $70,000 contract for Deonta Allen mural at Civic Center Apartments
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving a contract with artist Deonta Allen to design and install a public art mural at the Civic Center Apartments, a 49-unit permanent supportive housing development at 425 24th Street. The proposed contract is for up to $70,000, running from June 16, 2026 to June 30, 2027. To pay for this, Council would also adopt a resolution to take $73,742.49 from the Arts & Culture 1.5% Capital Improvements Program Fund, which holds developer-paid public art fees required by Richmond Municipal Code Section 6.50.020. The remaining appropriated funds may cover permitting, contingency, and project administration. Mural installation is anticipated by end of Summer 2026. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Deonta Allen mural contract

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $87,777 graffiti removal contract for General Warehouse
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with All Star Painting KD Inc. to remove graffiti from the historic concrete surfaces of the General Warehouse Building at the Point Potrero Marine Terminal. If approved, the contract would run until December 30, 2027, and cost no more than $87,777. The work includes graffiti removal tests at four locations, removal from all exterior surfaces, and application of anti-graffiti coating to 15,000 square feet, all within 20 working days of notice to proceed. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet and will be decided at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time. Watch for the council decision.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive April 2026 financial reports on investments, overtime, and taxes
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will receive April 2026 financial reports including an Investment and Cash Balance Report, overtime reports showing General Fund overtime at 87% of budget ($12.19M YTD), a Documentary Transfer Tax Report showing $513,926 in April 2026 revenue, and OPEB/Pension reports. Separately, Council will consider a resolution to place liens on 555 properties for $472,383.10 in unpaid garbage collection fees ($447,408.10 to Republic Services, $24,975 in City administrative fees). Property owners 5+ months delinquent receive a final notice and administrative hearing date before liens are placed on Contra Costa County property tax records.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Property owners with delinquent garbage fees should pay before the lien is recorded on their property tax records.

key_dates.label: Next garbage lien administrative hearing

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $247K contract for ballot measure study
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed sole-source contract with SCI Consulting Group for up to $247,000. Phase 1 ($97,000) covers feasibility, voter research, and political analysis. Phase 2 ($150,500) covers ballot-ready materials and outreach if Phase 1 finds a measure viable. The contract runs April 28, 2026 to June 30, 2027. The statutory deadline to place a measure on the November 2026 ballot is August 7, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could lead to a future ballot measure affecting your business or property.

key_dates.label: Statutory deadline to place measure on November 2026 ballot

Proposed SCI Consulting contract end date

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Place liens for unpaid garbage collection fees in Richmond
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resolution to place liens and special assessments on property tax records for households and businesses that have not paid their garbage collection service fees. The total outstanding amount is $472,383.10, covering 555 delinquent accounts, with $447,408.10 owed to Republic Services and $24,975.00 in City administrative fees. If approved, unpaid fees would be added to the owner's property tax bill, and failure to pay could eventually lead to loss of the property. This is a proposed decision that has not yet been voted on.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is on the preliminary lien list (Attachment 2) and pay any outstanding garbage fees before the council vote.

key_dates.label: Council vote on garbage lien resolution
Next administrative hearing for garbage liens

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on staffing and position control changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a resolution to delete one vacant Network and Systems Specialist II position ($235,932) and add one Jailer position ($136,402) in the Police Department, resulting in annual savings of approximately $99,530. The change is needed to maintain adequate staffing at the temporary holding facility and reduce officer time spent on booking trips to Martinez.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to expand Macdonald Avenue task force seats
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote to correct a May 19, 2026 action by adding one more seat to the Macdonald Avenue Corridor Task Force, bringing total new seats to five: three business representatives, one resident representative, and one flexible seat for a property owner, business owner, or both. Seats will be filled through the standard Boards and Commissions process; applicants must live, own a business, own property, or demonstrate a clear connection to the corridor. All appointments require City Council confirmation.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own a business or property on Macdonald Avenue, watch for the council vote — this could give you a chance to apply for a seat on the task force.

agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proclamation recognizing June 5, 2026 (the first Friday in June) as National Gun Violence Awareness Day, followed by Wear Orange Weekend on June 6–7. The proclamation is a symbolic declaration of support for raising awareness about gun violence and does not create any new laws, regulations, or business requirements.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive April 2026 crime report from Richmond Police
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council received the April 2026 monthly crime report from the Richmond Police Department. Violent crime fell 32% (121 to 82 incidents) and property crime fell 40% (420 to 252 incidents) compared to March 2026. Homicides rose from 0 to 2. Parking enforcement towed 125 vehicles (up 13%) and issued 5,239 parking citations. Three illegal dumping citations and two tobacco enforcement citations were issued. No new rules or enforcement changes were proposed.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to join regional stormwater compliance system
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to join the Contra Costa County Regional Alternative Compliance System. This system would give developers an off-site option to meet stormwater treatment rules when they cannot do the work on their own property. A proposed Joint Powers Agreement would create this shared program. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could affect your development project's stormwater compliance options.

ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a Joint Powers Agreement for participation in the Contra Costa County Regional Alternative Compliance System, which provides an off-site stormwater treatment compliance option for projects that cannot feasibly meet on-site stormwater treatment requirements; and AUTHORIZE the city manager, or their designee, to execute the agreement and any related documents necessary to implement the program – Public Works Department (Bradley Harms 510-307-8094).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $5.4M sewer repair contract with Veolia
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposal to authorize a task authorization (a formal work order) to Veolia Water West Operating Services, Inc. for sewer rehabilitation work. If approved, the contract would be worth up to $5,441,812 and would cover the repair or replacement of about two miles of sanitary sewer pipes as part of the Fiscal Year 2025-26 Grade 5 Risk Assessment Analysis Project. The amount includes a base cost of $4,534,843 plus a 20% contingency (extra funds set aside for unexpected costs). This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet and will be decided at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time. Watch for the council decision — this could affect local infrastructure and construction opportunities.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $700,000 fleet vehicle purchase
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a proposal to buy eight new fleet vehicles for the Port, Parks, and Abatement operations. The total cost would not exceed $700,000. The vehicles would be purchased from Dublin Chevrolet using an existing Alameda County master contract (a pre-approved bulk purchasing agreement that lets other governments buy at the same low price). This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet and will be decided at an upcoming council meeting.

If approved, this amendment would update maintenance responsibilities for new bicycle and pedestrian improvements at the Central Avenue and Interstate-80 interchange. The city manager would be authorized to sign the agreement. This is a proposed decision that has not yet been enacted.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Road closure at Glenn and McBryde for safer park access
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to close the direct vehicle connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue. This is part of the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project. The goal is to improve traffic safety, reduce cars cutting through the neighbourhood, and make it easier and safer for people to walk and bike to the park. If approved, the change would block cars from driving directly between the two streets, but pedestrians and cyclists would still be able to use the connection.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect local traffic patterns and your customers' access.

ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a roadway modification associated with the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project, including closure of the direct vehicular connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue to improve traffic safety, reduce cut-through traffic, and support pedestrian and bicycle connectivity – Public Works Department (Hillal Hamdan 510-621-1612/Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $750K in park construction cost overruns
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on a fourth amendment to the legal services agreement with Liebert Cassidy Whitmore, increasing the not-to-exceed amount by $400,000 to a total of $1,965,000 and extending the term through June 30, 2027. The firm provides labor and employment legal support, including negotiations for successor MOUs for three bargaining groups and the Fire Department's transition to Advanced Life Support services. Hourly rates are: partners $435, senior counsel $365, associates $260-$345, and paralegals $145.

key_dates.label: Council vote on LCW legal contract amendment
LCW contract extension through June 30, 2027 takes effect

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on BNSF railway crossing safety deal
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on approving a Grade Crossing Construction and Maintenance Agreement with Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railway (BNSF). This agreement would allow safety improvements at the Harbour Way South and Wright Avenue railroad crossing. If approved, the city would also pay for related costs like easements (the right to use a piece of land for a specific purpose) and licenses using grant money. The city manager would be authorized to sign the final documents.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time. Watch for the council decision.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $3.6M art and lighting safety contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on awarding a construction contract to FBD Vanguard Construction, Inc. for the Art and Lighting Safety and Accessibility Improvements Project. If approved, the contract would be for a total amount not to exceed $3,577,318. This includes a base bid of $3,252,107 plus a City-controlled contingency of $325,211 (equal to ten percent of the contract amount) to cover any unexpected costs. The project aims to improve safety and accessibility through art and lighting upgrades. Council will also consider authorizing the city manager to sign the contract and allowing the director of public works to approve change orders up to the contingency amount if needed.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time — monitor the council decision for potential impacts on nearby properties or construction.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on anti-discrimination plan for federal projects
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a resolution to adopt a Title VI Implementation Plan for the Public Works Department. Title VI is a federal law that bans discrimination based on race, color, or national origin in any program that gets federal money. If approved, this plan would set rules to make sure all federally funded public works projects in Richmond follow those nondiscrimination requirements. The plan covers how the city will handle complaints, train staff, and make sure everyone has fair access to these projects.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from business owners at this time. Watch for the council decision.

ADOPT a resolution approving the City of Richmond Public Works Department Title VI Implementation Plan to ensure compliance with federal nondiscrimination requirements for federally funded programs and projects - Public Works Department (Hillal Hamdan 510-621-1612).
